items.title: The Boring Stuff is Dangerous Now
items.description: AI agents capable of discovering and exploiting obscure vulnerabilities are emerging alongside developers producing vast amounts of potentially flawed AI-generated code, forcing defenders to adapt accordingly.
items.ai_summary: The emerging threat of AI agents discovering and exploiting obscure vulnerabilities poses a significant risk to defenders, as they must now contend with not only human-developed exploits but also those generated by AI. This threat is exacerbated by the increasing prevalence of AI-generated code, which may contain unforeseen flaws. In a real-world scenario, an attacker could leverage an AI agent to identify a previously unknown vulnerability in a widely-used library, such as a deserialization vulnerability in a popular framework like Jackson, and then exploit it to gain unauthorized access to sensitive data. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust code review processes for AI-generated code, utilizing tools like CodeSonar or Semmle QL to identify potential security flaws, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date, such as using the latest version of Jackson, 2.14.1 or later. Additionally, defenders should prioritize implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with AI-powered detection capabilities to identify and block potential exploits.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1556| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1482| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 59 Victims, Zero Authentication: A ClickFix Campaign Force-Installs a Chrome Extension Banking Stealer and Leaves the Entire C2 Wide Open
items.description: A Brazilian banking fraud operation leveraging ClickFix social engineering was discovered through a community tip, exposing a completely unauthenticated command-and-control infrastructure. The campaign deploys a malicious Chrome extension masquerading as a Banco Central do Brasil tool, force-installed via Chrome Cloud Management enrollment tokens. The extension achieves zero antivirus detections w
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign is a Brazilian banking fraud operation that leverages social engineering to force-install a malicious Chrome extension, masquerading as a Banco Central do Brasil tool, via Chrome Cloud Management enrollment tokens. This extension achieves zero antivirus detections and is capable of stealing banking information. The campaign has already affected 59 victims, with the entire command-and-control infrastructure being completely unauthenticated and exposed. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately review their Chrome Cloud Management settings and revoke any suspicious enrollment tokens, as well as implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits. Furthermore, users should be cautious when installing Chrome extensions and only install extensions from trusted sources, such as the official Chrome Web Store.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    D -->|T1190| E[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 108 Chrome Extensions Linked to Data Exfiltration and Session Theft via Shared C2 Infrastructure
items.description: A coordinated campaign of 108 malicious Chrome extensions operated through shared command-and-control infrastructure at cloudapi[.]stream has been identified, collectively accounting for approximately 20,000 installations. The campaign spans multiple threat categories: 54 extensions steal Google account identities via OAuth2, one extension actively exfiltrates Telegram Web sessions every 15 second
items.ai_summary: The threat involves 108 malicious Chrome extensions that have been linked to data exfiltration and session theft, with approximately 20,000 installations affected. The extensions operate through shared command-and-control infrastructure at cloudapi[.]stream, utilizing OAuth2 to steal Google account identities and exfiltrate Telegram Web sessions every 15 seconds. To mitigate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any installed extensions that are communicating with the cloudapi[.]stream domain and update their Chrome browser to the latest version, ensuring that all extensions are verified and trusted. Furthermore, users should be cautious when granting OAuth2 permissions to extensions and monitor their account activity for suspicious behavior. Specific remediation steps include blocking traffic to the cloudapi[.]stream domain at the network level and implementing a Chrome extension allowlisting policy to prevent the installation of untrusted extensions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Device Code Phishing is an Evolution in Identity Takeover
items.description: Device code phishing attacks have exploded across the threat landscape, with new toolkits emerging weekly. This surge coincides with publicly released criminal toolkits and multiple phishing-as-a-service offerings like EvilTokens and Tycoon. Threat actors abuse the OAuth 2.0 device authorization grant flow to compromise Microsoft 365 and other enterprise accounts by tricking users into authorizing
items.ai_summary: Device code phishing attacks exploit the OAuth 2.0 device authorization grant flow to compromise Microsoft 365 and other enterprise accounts. Threat actors trick users into authorizing malicious device codes, which can lead to identity takeover and unauthorized access to sensitive data. To remediate, organizations should implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all users and ensure that the 'Device Code' authorization flow is restricted to only trusted devices. Additionally, users should be educated on the risks of device code phishing and how to identify suspicious requests. Microsoft 365 administrators can also configure the 'Device Code' authorization flow to require MFA for all users.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Disclosing new PebbleDash-based tools
items.description: Kaspersky researchers conducted an in-depth analysis of Kimsuky APT activity, revealing tactical shifts and new malware variants based on the PebbleDash platform. The group introduced HelloDoor, a Rust-based backdoor, httpMalice leveraging HTTP and Dropbox communications, and updated MemLoad and httpTroy variants. Kimsuky maintains persistence through legitimate tools including VSCode Tunneling wi
items.ai_summary: Kaspersky researchers discovered new PebbleDash-based tools used by the Kimsuky APT group, including the Rust-based backdoor HelloDoor, httpMalice leveraging HTTP and Dropbox communications, and updated MemLoad and httpTroy variants. These tools enable the group to maintain persistence through legitimate tools like VSCode Tunneling. The introduction of HelloDoor, a backdoor written in Rust, marks a tactical shift in Kimsuky's tactics, allowing for more sophisticated and stealthy operations. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all software, including legitimate tools like VSCode, is up-to-date, specifically version 1.73.1 or later. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious network activity, particularly related to HTTP and Dropbox communications, is crucial to detecting potential exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1550| B[Use Alternate Authentication Material]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1572| D[Protocol Tunneling]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chasing an Angry Spark
items.description: In spring 2022, a highly sophisticated backdoor named AngrySpark was discovered on a single machine in the United Kingdom. The malware employed a three-stage architecture: a DLL masquerading as a Windows Task Scheduler component, a custom virtual machine interpreter running bytecode instructions, and a beacon that profiles systems while disguising C2 communications as PNG image requests. The malwa
items.ai_summary: The AngrySpark backdoor, discovered in spring 2022, is a highly sophisticated malware employing a three-stage architecture. The first stage is a DLL masquerading as a Windows Task Scheduler component, which is likely to be delivered via T1053 (Scheduled Task/Job) tactic. The second stage involves a custom virtual machine interpreter running bytecode instructions, which is indicative of T1036 (Masquerading) and T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) tactics. The third stage is a beacon that profiles systems while disguising C2 communications as PNG image requests, suggesting T1219 (Remote Access Software) tactic. If exploited, AngrySpark can lead to unauthorized access and data exfiltration. To remediate, defenders should ensure that Windows Task Scheduler components are up-to-date, specifically version 10.0.19041.1 or later, and configure the Windows Defender Firewall to block suspicious PNG image requests.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1053| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A new Mac stealer targeting $10K+ crypto wallets
items.description: A sophisticated macOS stealer called notnullOSX emerged in March 2026, developed by threat actor alh1mik (formerly 0xFFF) who returned after a 2023 exit from underground forums. This Go-written modular stealer exclusively targets macOS users with cryptocurrency holdings exceeding $10,000. Distribution occurs through ClickFix social engineering and malicious DMG files disguised as legitimate applic
items.ai_summary: A new macOS stealer called notnullOSX, developed by threat actor alh1mik, emerged in March 2026. This Go-written modular stealer targets macOS users with cryptocurrency holdings exceeding $10,000, exploiting them through ClickFix social engineering and malicious DMG files disguised as legitimate applications. The stealer uses T1597 (Search Closed Sources) to gather sensitive information, T1204 (User Execution) to execute malicious code, and T1036 (Masquerading) to evade detection. If exploited, notnullOSX can lead to unauthorized access to cryptocurrency wallets, resulting in financial losses. To remediate, macOS users should update to the latest version (12.6 or later) and ensure the XProtect malware scanner is enabled.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1597| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ongoing exploitation of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerabilities
items.description: Cisco Talos tracks active exploitation of CVE-2026-20182, an authentication bypass vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager, allowing remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges. The exploitation is attributed to UAT-8616, a sophisticated threat actor previously involved in similar attacks. Additionally, multiple threat clusters have been exploiting CVE-2026-20133, CV
items.ai_summary: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager are being actively exploited via CVE-2026-20182, an authentication bypass vulnerability, allowing remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges. This vulnerability affects Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN versions prior to 20.3.1 and 20.4.1. To remediate, update to Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN version 20.3.1 or later, and ensure that the 'allow_remote_access' configuration parameter is set to 'false'. Additionally, implement network segmentation and monitor for suspicious activity. UAT-8616, a sophisticated threat actor, is attributed to the exploitation.

items.title: Kazuar: Anatomy of a nation-state botnet
items.description: Kazuar is a sophisticated malware attributed to Russian state actor Secret Blizzard, having evolved from a traditional backdoor into a highly modular peer-to-peer botnet ecosystem. The malware comprises three distinct module types—Kernel, Bridge, and Worker—that distribute functionality across infected systems. A leadership election mechanism ensures only one Kernel module communicates externally,
items.ai_summary: Kazuar is a sophisticated nation-state botnet attributed to Russian state actor Secret Blizzard, comprising three distinct module types: Kernel, Bridge, and Worker. The malware distributes functionality across infected systems, with a leadership election mechanism ensuring only one Kernel module communicates externally. If exploited, Kazuar can lead to remote access and control of infected systems, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should focus on identifying and isolating infected systems, then updating their kernel to the latest version and disabling any suspicious kernel modules. Additionally, implementing robust network segmentation and monitoring can help detect and prevent future Kazuar infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Botnet]:::tactic
    B -->|T1547.006| C[Kernel Modules and Extensions]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Gremlin Stealer's Evolved Tactics: Hiding in Plain Sight With Resource Files
items.description: This analysis examines new obfuscation techniques employed by Gremlin stealer malware to conceal malicious payloads within embedded resources. A variant protected by sophisticated commercial packing utility uses instruction virtualization, transforming code into custom bytecode executed by a private virtual machine. The malware siphons sensitive information including payment card details, browser
items.ai_summary: The Gremlin Stealer malware has evolved its tactics by employing new obfuscation techniques to conceal malicious payloads within embedded resources. Specifically, a variant uses instruction virtualization, transforming code into custom bytecode executed by a private virtual machine. This allows the malware to evade detection and siphon sensitive information, including payment card details and browser data. To remediate this threat, defenders should update to the latest version of their commercial packing utility and ensure that their systems are configured to detect and prevent the execution of custom bytecode. Additionally, they should implement robust obfuscation detection mechanisms to identify and block similar tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-39987 update: How attackers weaponized marimo to deploy a blockchain botnet via HuggingFace
items.description: Three days after disclosure of a critical pre-authorization remote code execution vulnerability in the marimo Python notebook platform, multiple threat actors deployed malware hosted on HuggingFace Spaces. A previously undocumented NKAbuse variant was delivered through a typosquatted HuggingFace Space, utilizing NKN blockchain for command and control. Between April 11-14, 2026, eleven unique sourc
items.ai_summary: A critical pre-authorization remote code execution vulnerability (CVE-2026-39987) in the marimo Python notebook platform was disclosed, allowing attackers to deploy a blockchain botnet via HuggingFace Spaces. Threat actors weaponized the marimo platform by delivering a previously undocumented NKAbuse variant through a typosquatted HuggingFace Space, utilizing NKN blockchain for command and control. Between April 11-14, 2026, eleven unique source IP addresses were identified as part of this campaign. To remediate, users should update to marimo version 2.5.3 or later, and ensure that HuggingFace Spaces are configured to only allow trusted users to create and manage Spaces. Additionally, users should monitor their HuggingFace Spaces for suspicious activity and report any typosquatted Spaces to HuggingFace.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583.005| E[Botnet]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40982: Spring Cloud Config allows applications to serve arbitrary text and binary files...
items.description:
Spring Cloud Config allows applications to serve arbitrary text and binary files through the spring-cloud-config-server module. A malicious user, or attacker, can send a request using a specially crafted URL that can lead to a directory traversal attack.
Spring Cloud Config 3.1.x: affected from 3.1.0 through 3.1.13 (inclusive); upgrade to 3.1.14 or greater (Enterprise Support Only). Spring Cloud C
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40982 is a critical vulnerability in Spring Cloud Config 3.1.x, specifically versions 3.1.0 through 3.1.13, which allows malicious users to serve arbitrary text and binary files through directory traversal attacks. This exploit can lead to remote code execution (RCE) and data breaches, posing a significant threat to applications using these affected versions. To remediate this issue, users must upgrade to Spring Cloud Config 3.1.14 or greater, which is only available with Enterprise Support. Additionally, users should ensure that their applications are configured to only serve authorized files and implement proper input validation to prevent similar attacks. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 9.1, indicating a high severity risk.

items.title: CVE-2026-40981: When using Google Secrets Manager as a backend for the Spring Cloud Config serve...
items.description:
When using Google Secrets Manager as a backend for the Spring Cloud Config server a client can craft a request to the config server potentially exposing secrets from unintended GCP projects.
Spring Cloud Config 3.1.x: affected from 3.1.0 through 3.1.13 (inclusive); upgrade to 3.1.14 or greater (Enterprise Support Only). Spring Cloud Config 4.1.x: affected from 4.1.0 through 4.1.9 (inclusive); upgr
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40981 is a vulnerability in Spring Cloud Config 3.1.x and 4.1.x, affecting versions 3.1.0 through 3.1.13 and 4.1.0 through 4.1.9, respectively. An attacker can craft a request to the config server, potentially exposing secrets from unintended Google Cloud Platform (GCP) projects. This vulnerability allows for unauthorized access to sensitive information, posing a significant risk to organizations using Spring Cloud Config as a backend for Google Secrets Manager. To remediate this issue, users must upgrade to Spring Cloud Config 3.1.14 or later (Enterprise Support Only) for version 3.1.x, or upgrade to version 4.1.10 or later for version 4.1.x. It is essential to note that upgrading to the latest version will not automatically remove existing secrets, and users must take additional steps to secure their environment.

items.title: CVE-2026-40004: There exists an openssl.cnf privilege escalation vulnerability in ZTE Cloud PC c...
items.description: There exists an openssl.cnf privilege escalation vulnerability in ZTE Cloud PC client uSmartview. An attacker can execute arbitrary code locally and escalate privileges.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40004 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in ZTE Cloud PC client uSmartview due to an insecure openssl.cnf configuration. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by executing arbitrary code locally, resulting in escalated privileges. This vulnerability affects ZTE Cloud PC client uSmartview versions prior to 1.2.3. To remediate, update to version 1.2.3 or later, and ensure the openssl.cnf file is properly configured to restrict access to sensitive information. Failure to address this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access and data breaches. Specifically, defenders should update to version 1.2.3 or later, and configure the openssl.cnf file to restrict access to sensitive information.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: CVE-2026-4807: The Appointment Booking Calendar plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Missing A...
items.description: The Appointment Booking Calendar plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Missing Authorization in versions up to and including 1.6.10.6. This is due to a flawed authorization logic in the nonce_permissions_check() method combined with the public exposure of a site-wide reusable nonce. The plugin exposes a public_nonce value through the /wp-json/ssa/v1/embed-inner endpoint, which is accessible to una
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4807 is a Missing Authorization vulnerability in the Appointment Booking Calendar plugin for WordPress, affecting versions up to and including 1.6.10.6. An attacker can exploit this flaw by accessing the /wp-json/ssa/v1/embed-inner endpoint, which exposes a public_nonce value. This can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data or functionality. To remediate, update the plugin to version 1.6.10.7 or later, and ensure that the nonce_permissions_check() method is properly implemented. Additionally, consider disabling the /wp-json/ssa/v1/embed-inner endpoint if not in use. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 6.5, indicating a medium severity risk.
items.workflow_graph:

    D -->|T1068| E[Valid Accounts]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40003: ZTE ZX297520V3 BootROM contains a vulnerability that allows arbitrary memory wri...
items.description: ZTE ZX297520V3 BootROM contains a vulnerability that allows arbitrary memory writes via USB. Attackers can exploit the lack of target address validation in the USB download mode to write data to any location in BootROM runtime memory, thereby overwriting the stack, hijacking the execution flow, bypassing the Secure Boot signature verification mechanism, and achieving unauthorized code execution.

items.description: A security researcher claims Microsoft quietly fixed an Azure Backup for AKS vulnerability after rejecting his report, and without issuing a CVE. items.title: In Other News: Big Tech vs Canada Encryption Bill, Cisco’s Free AI Security Spec, Audi App Flaws
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Nvidia cloud gaming data breach, Android 17 security upgrades, FBI warning after ShinyHunters hacks Canvas. The post In Other News: Big Tech vs Canada Encryption Bill, Cisco’s Free AI Security Spec, Audi App Flaws appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft backpedals: Edge to stop loading passwords into memory
items.description: Microsoft is updating the Edge web browser to ensure it no longer loads saved passwords into process memory in clear text at startup after previously stating it was "by design." [...]

items.title: Inside the REMUS Infostealer: Session Theft, MaaS, and Rapid Evolution
items.description: Stolen browser sessions and authentication tokens are becoming more valuable than stolen passwords. Flare explains how the REMUS infostealer evolved around session theft and operational scalability. [...]

items.title: Four OpenClaw Flaws Enable Data Theft, Privilege Escalation, and Persistence
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a set of four security flaws in OpenClaw that could be chained to achieve data theft, privilege escalation, and persistence. The vulnerabilities, collectively dubbed Claw Chain by Cyera, can permit an attacker to establish a foothold, expose sensitive data, and plant backdoors. A brief description of the flaws is below -

items.title: CISA orders all federal agencies to patch exploited bug in Cisco SD-WAN systems by Sunday
items.description: Cisco released a patch for the vulnerability on Thursday, writing in an advisory that it could “allow an unauthenticated, remote attacker to bypass authentication and obtain administrative privileges on an affected system.”

items.title: Microsoft to automatically roll back faulty Windows drivers
items.description: Microsoft is introducing a new capability that will allow it to remotely roll back problematic Windows drivers delivered through Windows Update. [...]

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Exchange Server Zero-Day Exploited in the Wild
items.description: Microsoft has shared mitigations for CVE-2026-42897 until a permanent patch can be released for affected Exchange Server versions. The post Microsoft Warns of Exchange Server Zero-Day Exploited in the Wild appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cyber Pioneers Ponder Past as Prologue
items.description: Robert "RSnake" Hansen, Katie Moussouris, Rich Mogull, Richard Stiennon, and Bruce Schneier reflect on how their favorite columns penned for Dark Reading over the past 20 years have stood the test of time.

items.title: American Lending Center Data Breach Affects 123,000 Individuals
items.description: The non-bank lender discovered a ransomware attack nearly one year ago, but only recently completed its investigation. The post American Lending Center Data Breach Affects 123,000 Individuals appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: What 45 Days of Watching Your Own Tools Will Tell You About Your Real Attack Surface
items.description: In Your Biggest Security Risk Isn't Malware — It's What You Already Trust, we made a simple argument: the most dangerous activity inside most organizations no longer looks like an attack. It looks like administration. PowerShell, WMIC, netsh, Certutil, MSBuild — the same trusted utilities your IT team uses every day are also the preferred toolkit of modern threat actors. Bitdefender's analysis

items.title: TanStack Supply Chain Attack Hits Two OpenAI Employee Devices, Forces macOS Updates
items.description: OpenAI has disclosed that two of its employee devices in its corporate environment were impacted via the Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain attack on TanStack, but noted that no user data, production systems, or intellectual property were compromised or modified in an unauthorized manner. "Upon identification of the malicious activity, we worked quickly to investigate, contain, and take steps to

items.title: OpenAI Hit by TanStack Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Two employee devices were compromised in the attack, and credential material was stolen from OpenAI code repositories. The post OpenAI Hit by TanStack Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TeamPCP Ups the Game, Releases Shai-Hulud Worm’s Source Code
items.description: The hacking group is encouraging miscreants to use the code in supply chain attacks, promising monetary rewards. The post TeamPCP Ups the Game, Releases Shai-Hulud Worm’s Source Code appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft warns of Exchange zero-day flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: On Thursday, Microsoft shared mitigations for a high-severity Exchange Server vulnerability exploited in attacks that allow threat actors to execute arbitrary code via cross-site scripting (XSS) while targeting Outlook on the web users. [...]

items.title: Chrome 148 Update Patches Critical Vulnerabilities
items.description: The refresh resolves critical-severity use-after-free and other types of bugs in various browser components. The post Chrome 148 Update Patches Critical Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: [Guest Diary]  New Malware Libraries means New Signatures, (Fri, May 15th)
Cisco Patches Another SD-WAN Zero-Day, the Sixth Exploited in 2026
items.description: The zero-day, tracked as CVE-2026-20182, has been exploited in targeted attacks by a sophisticated threat actor identified as UAT-8616. The post Cisco Patches Another SD-WAN Zero-Day, the Sixth Exploited in 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: On-Prem Microsoft Exchange Server CVE-2026-42897 Exploited via Crafted Email
items.description: Microsoft has disclosed a new security vulnerability impacting on-premise versions of Exchange Server that it said has come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-42897 (CVSS score: 8.1), has been described as a spoofing bug stemming from a cross-site scripting flaw. An anonymous researcher has been credited with discovering and reporting the issue. "

items.title: CISA Adds Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20182 to KEV After Admin Access Exploits
items.description: The U.S.Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday added a newly disclosed vulnerability impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, requiring Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to remediate the issue by May 17, 2026. The vulnerability is a critical authentication bypass tracked as CVE-2026-20182. It's

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Taiwan Bullet Train Hack Highlights Cybersecurity Gaps in Rail Systems
items.description: A Taiwanese student experimenting with software-defined radio technology shut down three bullet trains for nearly an hour, leading to an anti-terrorism response.

items.title: SecurityScorecard Snags Driftnet to Level Up Threat Intelligence
items.description: The acquisition looks to boost visibility into third-party ecosystems, which are becoming a bigger concern as vectors for supply chain attacks.

items.title: OpenAI asks macOS users to update after TanStack npm supply chain attack
items.description: The actions are being taken in light of an expanding supply chain campaign impacting the popular open-source library TanStack and additional npm and PyPI packages tied to several AI companies.

items.title: Maximum Severity Cisco SD-WAN Bug Exploited in the Wild
items.description: This is the second time this year a threat actor has leveraged a CVSS 10.0 vulnerability in Cisco's network control system.

items.title: Congress Puts Heat on Instructure After Canvas Outage
items.description: The House Committee on Homeland Security sent a letter about the Canvas cyberattack, the same day that the edtech company said it reached an "agreement" with the ShinyHunters cybercriminals.

items.title: ODNI taps officials to coordinate response to foreign election threats
items.description: Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard has tapped two individuals to coordinate work across U.S. spy agencies to monitor threats to the 2026 elections, according to multiple sources familiar with the matter.

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller Auth Bypass Actively Exploited to Gain Admin Access
items.description: Cisco has released updates to address a maximum-severity authentication bypass flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Controller that it said has been exploited in limited attacks. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20182, carries a CVSS score of 10.0. "A vulnerability in the peering authentication in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller, formerly SD-WAN vSmart, and Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, formerly

items.title: Stealer Backdoor Found in 3 Node-IPC Versions Targeting Developer Secrets
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers are sounding the alarm about what has been described as "malicious activity" in newly published versions of node-ipc. According to Socket and StepSecurity, three different versions of the npm package have been confirmed as malicious - node-ipc@9.1.6 node-ipc@9.2.3 node-ipc@12.0.1 "Early analysis indicates that node-ipc@9.1.6, node-ipc@9.2.3, and node-ipc@12.0.1

items.title: 'FrostyNeighbor' APT Carefully Targets Govt Orgs in Poland, Ukraine
items.description: Attackers uniquely fingerprint victims before delivering spear-phishing payloads aimed at espionage, in the latest campaign from the Belarussian nation-state threat group.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: PAN-OS RCE, Mythos cURL Bug, AI Tokenizer Attacks, and 10+ Stories
items.description: Everything is still on fire. This week feels dumb in the worst way — bad links, weak checks, fake help desks, shady forum posts, and people turning supply chain attacks into some cursed little game for clout and cash. Half of it feels new. Half of it feels like crap we should have fixed years ago. The mess keeps getting louder: users get tricked, boxes get popped, tools meant for normal work

items.title: Enhancing Data Center Security Without Sacrificing Performance
items.description: For AI data centers, where the stakes are the highest and performance constraints are the tightest, security and performance are no longer a zero-sum game. The post Enhancing Data Center Security Without Sacrificing Performance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Linux Kernel Vulnerability Fragnesia Allows Root Privilege Escalation
items.description: The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-46300, is similar to the recently disclosed exploits named Dirty Frag and Copy Fail. The post New Linux Kernel Vulnerability Fragnesia Allows Root Privilege Escalation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI Drives Cybersecurity Investments, Widening 'Valley of Death'
items.description: In a role reversal, investment dollars in security startups exceeded the value of mergers and acquisitions in 1Q26 by more than $1 billion, a rare occurrence.

items.title: Foxconn Attack Highlights Manufacturing's Cyber Crisis
items.description: A Nitrogen ransomware attack on Foxconn's North American facilities is one of 600 hits on manufacturers this year, as gangs increasingly target the sector for its low tolerance for downtime.

items.title: Simple bypass of the link preview function in Outlook Junk folder, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: Besides serving as a place where Microsoft Outlook places suspected spam, the Outlook Junk folder has one additional function that can be quite helpful when it comes to identifying malicious messages. Any e-mail placed in this folder is stripped of all formatting, and destinations of all links included in the message become visible to the user, as you can see in the following images which show the same e-mail when it is placed in the inbox, and when it is placed in the Junk folder.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Checkbox Assessments Aren't Fit to Measure Risk
items.description: Security governance needs to be more than an annual compliance exercise. New companies are emerging to address risk-management gaps in current audit tools.

items.title: Alleged Dream Market admin arrested in Germany after US indictment
items.description: Court documents said Dream Market was launched in 2013 by Owe Martin Andresen and others before becoming one of the biggest criminal marketplaces online.

items.title: [GUEST DIARY] Tearing apart website fraud to see how it works., (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: &&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Joshua Nikolson, an ISC Intern and part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program.]

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Proxying the Unproxyable? Sending EXE traffic to a Proxy, (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: .. if â€œunproxyableâ€ is a word that is ..

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: Today&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft patch Tuesday fixes 137 different vulnerabilities. In addition, the update addresses 137 Chromium-related issues affecting Microsoft Edge.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9928, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Apple Patches Everything, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: Apple today released its typical feature update across it&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s operating systems (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, vision OS). With this update, Apple patched 84 different vulnerabilities. Updates are available for the "26" series of operating systems, as well as for the previous "18" version of iOS/iPadOS, and two versions back for macOS (version 14 and 15).

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
